stem: The alternating series \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{k+1}\,[[c]]}{k^{[[p]]}} \) is approximated by the partial sum of its first \( [[N]] \) terms. By the alternating series estimation theorem, the error is at most the first omitted term. What is that error bound?
solution: The first omitted term is the \( ([[N]]+1) \)th term, so the error bound is \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{([[N]]+1)^{[[p]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the \( [[N]] \)th term instead of the first omitted \( ([[N]]+1) \)th term.
This uses the \( ([[N]]+2) \)th term, skipping one term too far.
This raises the denominator to the wrong power \( [[p]]+1 \).
This doubles the correct bound.
This uses the wrong power \( [[p]]-1 \) on the denominator.
This is off by one from the correct bound.

stem: The alternating series \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{k+1}\,[[c]]}{k!} \) is approximated by the partial sum of its first \( [[N]] \) terms. By the alternating series estimation theorem, the error is at most the first omitted term. What is that error bound?
solution: The first omitted term is the \( ([[N]]+1) \)th term, so the error bound is \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{([[N]]+1)!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the \( [[N]] \)th term's factorial instead of the \( ([[N]]+1) \)th.
This uses the \( ([[N]]+2) \)th term, skipping one term too far.
This replaces the factorial \( ([[N]]+1)! \) with just \( [[N]]+1 \).
This doubles the correct bound.
This is off by one from the correct bound.
This is off by one from the correct bound.

stem: The alternating series \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} (-1)^{k+1}\,[[c]]\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{k} \) has terms decreasing geometrically. It is approximated by the partial sum of its first \( [[N]] \) terms. By the alternating series estimation theorem, the error is at most the first omitted term. What is that error bound?
solution: The first omitted term is the \( ([[N]]+1) \)th term, so the error bound is \( [[c]]\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{[[N]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the \( [[N]] \)th term instead of the first omitted \( ([[N]]+1) \)th term.
This uses the \( ([[N]]+2) \)th term, skipping one term too far.
This doubles the correct bound.
This inverts the common ratio \( p/q \).
This is off by one from the correct bound.
This is off by one from the correct bound.

stem: The alternating series \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{k+1}}{k^{[[p]]}} \) is approximated by a partial sum of its first \( n \) terms. By the alternating series estimation theorem, the error using \( n \) terms is at most the \( (n+1) \)th term. What is the smallest number of terms \( n \) that guarantees an error less than \( \dfrac{1}{[[K]]} \)?
solution: We need the \( (n+1) \)th term to satisfy \( \dfrac{1}{(n+1)^{[[p]]}} < \dfrac{1}{[[K]]} \), i.e. \( (n+1)^{[[p]]} \ge [[K]] \). Since \( [[K]] = ([[N0]]+1)^{[[p]]} \), the smallest such \( n \) is \( n = \sqrt[[[p]]]{[[K]]} - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract \( 1 \), reporting \( n+1 \) instead of \( n \).
This is off by one in the other direction.
This reports the threshold \( [[K]] \) itself instead of solving for \( n \).
This is off by one from the correct number of terms.
This is off by one from the correct number of terms.
This is off by two from the correct number of terms.
